name: Avalon
longDescription: The Otherworld - Celtic Britain The Isle of Apples - Where King Arthur Awaits
shortDescription: The Otherworld - Celtic Britain The Isle of Apples - Where King Arthur Awaits
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance: The Otherworld - Celtic Britain The Isle of Apples - Where King Arthur Awaits.

geography: A hidden island shrouded in mist; located somewhere beyond the mundane world (often associated with Glastonbury Tor); contains healing apples and Otherworldly abundance
relatedEvents: Arthur's wounding at Camlann and journey to Avalon
Lady of the Lake delivering Excalibur
Sleeping King Arthur awaiting return during Britain's greatest need
Binding of the Grail at Avalon

keyMyths.title: The Wounding of Arthur and Arrival in Avalon
keyMyths.description: Following his final battle at Camlann, the mortally wounded King Arthur is taken to Avalon, a mystical island of healing, by Morgan le Fay and other magical women. He is brought there to recover from his wounds, with the promise of eventual return, though the timing remains uncertain.

keyMyths.title: Avalon as Isle of Apples (Insula Avallonis)
keyMyths.description: Avalon is described as an island abundant with apple trees, a paradise-like place where crops grow without cultivation and the inhabitants live long lives. This association with apples links Avalon to Celtic traditions of the Otherworld and immortality.

keyMyths.title: Morgan le Fay's Rule in Avalon
keyMyths.description: Morgan le Fay is often depicted as the ruler or high priestess of Avalon, wielding powerful magic and possessing knowledge of healing arts. She is responsible for Arthur's care and the overall well-being of the island and its inhabitants.
keyMyths.source: Various Arthurian Romances, including Chrétien de Troyes and later works

keyMyths.title: The Forging of Excalibur
keyMyths.description: While some accounts place the Lady of the Lake as the giver of Excalibur, other traditions suggest the sword was forged or kept on Avalon. This connects the island to the symbols of Arthurian kingship and the magical origin of the legendary blade.

keyMyths.title: Passage to Avalon Through Glastonbury
keyMyths.description: Glastonbury in Somerset, England, is often identified as the physical location of Avalon, with the Tor acting as a gateway to the island. Legends claim the Holy Grail was brought to Glastonbury, further linking it to Arthurian lore and the spiritual significance of Avalon.
keyMyths.source: William of Malmesbury, *De Antiquitate Glastoniensis Ecclesiae*

sources.name: Geoffrey of Monmouth, *Historia Regum Britanniae* (c. 1136)
sources.description: A highly influential, though largely fictionalized, history of Britain that first introduces Avalon as the place where Arthur is taken after his final battle.
sources.title: Geoffrey of Monmouth, *Historia Regum Britanniae* (c. 1136)

sources.name: Geoffrey of Monmouth, *Vita Merlini* (c. 1150)
sources.description: A poem attributed to Geoffrey of Monmouth that further develops the image of Avalon as the Isle of Apples, ruled by magical women.
sources.title: Geoffrey of Monmouth, *Vita Merlini* (c. 1150)

sources.name: Robert de Boron, *Merlin* (late 12th century)
sources.description: An early Arthurian romance that explores Merlin's role and often links magical objects and places to the Arthurian narrative.
sources.title: Robert de Boron, *Merlin* (late 12th century)

sources.name: Chrétien de Troyes, Arthurian Romances (late 12th century)
sources.description: A collection of Arthurian romances by a French author that helped popularize many Arthurian legends and characters, though Avalon's role is less prominent.
sources.title: Chrétien de Troyes, Arthurian Romances (late 12th century)

sources.name: William of Malmesbury, *De Antiquitate Glastoniensis Ecclesiae* (c. 1130)
sources.description: A historical account of Glastonbury Abbey that connects the site to Arthurian legends and the possible location of Avalon.
sources.title: William of Malmesbury, *De Antiquitate Glastoniensis Ecclesiae* (c. 1130)
